title: Runtime and Execution Model

learningObjectives.text: Explain high-level javascript model using a new example and justify the result.
Explain engine and runtime using a new example and justify the result.
Explain execution contexts using a new example and justify the result.
Explain call stack using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in runtime and execution model recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **How JavaScript Works** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **High-level JavaScript model** → **Engine and runtime** → **Execution contexts** → **Call stack**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: High-level JavaScript model
sections.body: An engine parses and executes language code; the runtime adds host APIs and asynchronous scheduling. Keeping those layers distinct explains how the same language runs in different environments.

sections.title: Engine and runtime
sections.body: The engine evaluates source and manages execution. A runtime combines the engine with host APIs, an event loop, and supporting infrastructure.

sections.title: Execution contexts
sections.body: An execution context contains the information required to run a unit of code, including bindings, scope relationships, and a this binding. Function calls create additional contexts.

sections.title: Call stack
sections.body: The call stack records active execution contexts. Calls push frames and returns pop them. Long synchronous work blocks later JavaScript and delays queued work.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Each call pushes an execution context until the innermost function returns.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Runtime and Execution Model connects High-level JavaScript model, Engine and runtime, Execution contexts.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which change is the best fix?

choices.label: Delete the stack trace and add random delays.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Runtime and Execution Model rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Read the stack from the thrown error through its callers, then inspect the first application frame that violates the expected input.
choices.feedback: Read the stack from the thrown error through its callers, then inspect the first application frame that violates the expected input.

choices.label: Catch the error silently at every function boundary.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Runtime and Execution Model rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Rename both functions without inspecting the input.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Runtime and Execution Model rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Read the stack from the thrown error through its callers, then inspect the first application frame that violates the expected input.
questions.hint: Choose the smallest change that fixes the underlying model, not a workaround that hides the symptom.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Runtime and Execution Model?

choices.label: Use the Runtime and Execution Model mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.
choices.feedback: Use the Runtime and Execution Model mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.

choices.label: Use Runtime and Execution Model in every file because more abstraction always improves software.

choices.label: Avoid Runtime and Execution Model completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Runtime and Execution Model rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Runtime and Execution Model rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use the Runtime and Execution Model mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.

assessmentProfile.label: Mental model plus practical code reasoning
assessmentProfile.rationale: The topic combines an important model with implementation consequences, so both explanation and code reasoning are required.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
